Introduction
In every decade, a revolution in Computer Science has brought a profound shift in how the world works. Consider the arc: the transistor in 1940s (which made computers small), the microprocessor in 1970s, the ARPANET in 1969 that gave rise to the modern Internet, the World Wide Web that Tim Berners-Lee layered on top of it in 1989, desktop computers like Apple’s Macintosh in 1980s and Windows OS in 90s from Microsoft.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Technological Advancements (left to right): the Transistor, the Microprocessor, the Internet, Apple’s Macintosh, and Windows 95 alongside classic Mac OSFurther, the dot-com bubble at the turn of the millennium revolutionized the coming 21st Century, followed by the iPhone in 2007 pioneering the smartphone revolution. Then Android and iOS drove the smartphone boom into the early 2010s, and now Artificial Intelligence (AI) reaching everyone through applications like OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Google’s Gemini, Anthropic’s Claude and many other open-source models. Each of these shifts has left its mark on the world in its own way.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

(Left to right) The iPhone in 2007, followed by the iOS and Android logosThis decade is being shaped by AI models in a similar fashion, and they’re proving useful in more than one direction. For software engineers, they’ve become a genuine helping hand or a buddy available 24/7, 365 days helping in the tasks: writing small snippets of code for an application, explaining or navigating an unfamiliar codebase, debugging by working through logs, writing test cases, and more. Beyond engineers, non-technical people are using the same models for their own everyday needs: summarizing long documents, drafting emails, brainstorming ideas, generating images, and so on. It’s why ’s now-famous line still holds up: “AI is the new electricity”. With AI woven this deeply into our day-to-day life (whether personal or professional), a thousand possibilities have opened up. To understand about Generative AI, we need to first understand what AI is.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Artificial Intelligence (AI)In this 5 part blog series, I will be discussing about AI, Generative AI and the 20 important concepts (4 each in this 5 part blog series) of AI that every software engineer should know. If you want to understand any particular concept in detail, then you can refer to the references that I have cited at the end of each blog.
In Part 1 of the 5-part blog series, I will be discussing about introduction of AI, ML, Deep Learning, Generative AI, followed by first 4 AI Concepts: Large Language Models (LLMs), Tokenization, Vectorization and Attention Mechanism. Let’s get started :)
What’s Actually Inside “AI”?
Before we get to Generative AI (Gen AI), let’s back up one step and understand: What is AI, actually?
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the umbrella term for technology that enables computers and machines to simulate distinct human abilities: learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy. In practice, that means building computational systems that can do things that used to require a human mind: recognizing patterns, understanding language, making judgment calls, and learning from experience.
Here’s the part most people get wrong: AI isn’t a recent invention now known to the world population, AI has been around for a very long time. It just feels brand new because of apps like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini: but scientists have been chasing “thinking machines” since the 1950s, over 70 years of formal research before any of us ever typed a prompt.
Directly beneath AI sits Machine Learning (ML). ML involves creating models by training an algorithm through either of the techniques: supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or self-supervised learning, and more: on data until it can make predictions or decisions on its own. That training works by adjusting the model’s internal parameters as it learns, rather than following rules a human explicitly programmed in. When set up correctly, this helps you make predictions that minimize the errors that arise from merely guessing.
One level deeper is Deep Learning: a subset of ML built on multi-layered neural networks, whose design has been based like structure of the human brain. It’s the workhorse behind most state-of-the-art AI today, from computer vision and generative AI to self-driving cars and robotics, it automates most of the feature-extraction work that used to require a human to hand-engineer.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A representation of Neural Network (YouTube: 3Blue1Brown)So, what’s actually going on inside one of these neural networks?
A neural network is a computational structure inspired by the brain: interconnected layers of nodes (“neurons”). Data flows in through an input layer, gets reshaped through one or more hidden layers of mathematical transformations, and comes out the other side as an output. Every connection between nodes carries a weight: a number that gets nudged during training to make the network’s predictions a little less wrong each time.
The Deep Learning finally brings us to Generative AI: GenAI makes it possible to create new content, ranging from text to images, videos, speech and even music. It can thus achieve realistic and personal interactions between humans and machines. It learns patterns from massive datasets of human creations to generate new text, images, music, code, and video that look or sound like a human made them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI applications (OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Anthropic’s Claude, Google’s Gemini)Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI, Machine Learning, Deep Learning, and Generative AI, shown as nested layers (Diagram: Data Couch)4 Core AI Concepts
As I mentioned in the intro, this post covers the first 4 of 20 core AI concepts: the kind that come in handy whether you’re actually building with AI or just holding your own in a workplace conversation about it. Here’s what’s ahead:
Large Language Models (LLMs)
A Large Language Model is a deep neural network trained on enormous amounts of text data - books, websites, code, articles, etc, with the sole goal of predicting “what comes next” in an input sequence. In simple terms, LLMs are neural networks trained on massive text data to predict the next token of an input sequence. For example: given “The sky is ___,” it predicts “blue” and through this simple objective, the model internalizes grammar, reasoning patterns, and nuance. At inference time, it generates responses one token at a time — each token chosen based on probability given all previous tokens.
“Large” refers to the sheer number of parameters — the adjustable weights inside the network. GPT-2 had 1.5 billion parameters, a figure OpenAI has confirmed directly. Beyond that, things get murkier: neither OpenAI nor Anthropic has ever officially disclosed parameter counts for GPT-4, GPT-5 or any Claude model. So it’s fairer to say modern models like GPT-4 or Claude are estimated to range from hundreds of billions to well over a trillion parameters.
Tokenization
Tokenization is the process of breaking text into smaller units called tokens before feeding it into an LLM. A token is not exactly a word — it’s a subword chunk. For example, “tokenization” might become [“token”, “ization”], and “unbelievable” might split into [“un”, “believ”, “able”]. This is done using algorithms like Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), which learns common subword patterns from training data and builds a vocabulary.
Why does this matter practically? Because LLMs are billed and limited by tokens, not words. The model’s context window (how much it can read at once) is also measured in tokens — Claude’s 200K context means ~150,000 words. Too many input tokens = higher cost and slower responses.
Tokenization examples across different words ()As for LLM, it is difficult to understand natural human language, hence it is essential for it to break the text into smaller words / tokens. Tokens tell the model what to focus on: the smallest unit it can derive meaning from. And to derive the meaning of those tokens, this covers by the process knows as Vectorization.
Vectorization
Vectorization (also called embeddings) converts tokens into a list of floating-point numbers - a vector (of n-dimension in an n-dimensional space) - which represents its meaning in a high-dimensional mathematical space. The key property: semantically similar text produces geometrically close vectors. The process of mapping of a word in n-dimensional space such that all nearby words / similar meaning words are clustered together and opposite meaning words are somewhere far away, is called as Vectorization.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A real vector space example: semantically similar concepts (Chicken, Wolf, Dog, Cat) cluster together, while unrelated ones (Banana, Apple) sit elsewhere (Reference: Google)Example: if you embed “Mumbai weather today” and “What is the climate in Bombay?”, their vectors will be much closer to each other than either is to “How do I sort a Java list?” You measure that closeness using cosine similarity — the cosine of the angle between two vectors (1.0 = identical direction, 0 = orthogonal/unrelated, -1 = opposite direction). This is the mathematical engine behind semantic search, RAG, and recommendation systems. Models like text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI) or embed-english-v3.0 (Cohere) are dedicated embedding models — their only job is to produce these vectors, not generate text.
Example of Vectorization (illustrative only, not real embedding outpu)The end result: the LLM can now break text into tokens and understand the meaning of those tokens as coordinates in its vocabulary space — which is what lets it construct sentences effectively.
Now we have an LLM that can tokenize input text and convert it into vectors. There’s one more concept that changed the entire industry and made LLMs what they are today: the Attention Mechanism.
Attention Mechanism
All input tokens in an LLM are converted into numerical vectors. But what about those words that are same in pronunciation but they mean differently as per the sentence? Before attention (in older Recurrent Neural Network (RNNs)), models processed text sequentially — word by word — which made it hard to connect distant words. Attention solves this by letting every word in a sentence look at every other word simultaneously and decide how much to “pay attention” to each one when building its understanding. In simple words, after tokenizing the words from the input passed to the LLM, how does the LLM process the ambiguous words? That’s when attention mechanism comes into play. The Attention mechanism is the core innovation that makes modern AI powerful.
The Attention mechanism (“Attention is all you need”, 2017) is a specialised mathematical operation performed on the ambiguous context vectors with the inherent context vectors, which then shifts the vector of an ambiguous term closer to the vectors of its contextual modifiers.
Example: The word “apple” can mean either a fruit or a tech company. Apple + “revenue” → shifts the vector toward the company category (alongside Google, Meta). Apple + “tasty” → shifts the vector toward the food category (alongside banana, guava). This is how the attention mechanism adds context to resolve ambiguity.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How the Attention Mechanism resolves ambiguity, step by step, using the “apple” exampleHow it works:

• Addressing Ambiguity: Many words, like “apple,” have different meanings depending on the context (e.g., a fruit vs. the tech company). The attention mechanism looks at nearby, contextual words in a sentence to determine the specific intended meaning.
